questions.question: Explain the difference between array and linked list. Give two advantages and two disadvantages of linked lists.
keyPoints: Array: contiguous memory, fixed size; Linked list: non-contiguous, dynamic size
Advantages: Dynamic size, efficient insertions/deletions at beginning, no memory wastage
Disadvantages: No random access (O(n) traversal), extra memory for pointers, not cache-friendly

questions.question: Write an algorithm to insert a node at the beginning of a singly linked list.
keyPoints: Create a new node using malloc/new
Assign data to new node
Set new node's next to current head
Update head pointer to new node
Handle empty list case

questions.question: What are the different types of linked lists? Explain briefly.
keyPoints: Singly Linked List: nodes have only next pointer
Doubly Linked List: nodes have both next and previous pointers
Circular Linked List: last node points back to first node
Header node: dummy node at beginning for simplification

questions.question: Explain the concept of traversal in linked lists with an example.
keyPoints: Traversal: visiting each node exactly once
Start from head node
Follow next pointers until NULL is reached
Process data at each node
Time complexity: O(n)

questions.question: What are the time complexities of the following operations in a singly linked list? (a) Insertion at beginning (b) Insertion at end (c) Deletion at beginning (d) Search for an element
keyPoints: (a) Insertion at beginning: O(1)
(b) Insertion at end: O(n) - requires traversal
(c) Deletion at beginning: O(1)
(d) Search: O(n) in worst case

questions.question: Define a node structure for a singly linked list and doubly linked list.
keyPoints: Singly node: data field + next pointer
Doubly node: data field + next pointer + previous pointer
Each node typically implemented as struct in C/C++
Pointers store memory addresses of next/previous nodes

questions.question: What is a circular linked list? Differentiate between singly and doubly circular linked lists.
keyPoints: Circular LL: last node points to first node (no NULL)
Singly circular: each node has next pointer only, last.next = head
Doubly circular: nodes have next and prev, last.next = head, first.prev = last
Both eliminate end-of-list NULL check

questions.question: Write a C program to create a singly linked list with 5 nodes and display all elements.
keyPoints: Define node structure with data and next pointer
Create nodes using malloc
Link nodes by setting next pointers
Display using traversal loop
Free memory at the end
Complete code with main function

questions.question: Write an algorithm to delete a node from a singly linked list given the position. Handle all cases.
keyPoints: Handle deletion at: (1) beginning - update head
(2) middle/end - find previous node
(3) Handle empty list
(4) Handle invalid position
Use pointers: prev, current, temp
Update next pointers correctly
Free deleted node memory

questions.question: Explain how linked lists can be used to implement Stack and Queue data structures. Write pseudo-code for push and pop operations of a stack using linked list.
keyPoints: Stack: LIFO - insert/delete at head (top)
Queue: FIFO - insert at tail, delete at head
Stack using LL: push = insert at beginning, pop = delete from beginning
Queue using LL: enqueue = insert at end, dequeue = delete from beginning
Advantages over arrays: dynamic size

questions.question: Write a function to reverse a singly linked list. Explain the algorithm and its time/space complexity.
keyPoints: Three pointers technique: prev, current, next
Initialize: prev = NULL, current = head
Iterate: while current != NULL, store next, reverse pointer, move prev and current
At end: set head = prev
Time: O(n), Space: O(1) - in-place reversal
